senses.definition: plural of refractor; devices or substances that cause light to change direction or to focus by refraction.
examples: The telescope uses several refractors to collect distant light.
Glass refractors can focus sunlight onto a small spot.

senses.definition: difficult to control; stubborn or unmanageable.
examples: The patient was in a refractory mood and wouldn't cooperate.
Refractory metal alloys resist high temperatures.

senses.definition: a material that resists heat or doesn't burn easily; a substance that is resistant to heat.
examples: The reactor uses a refractory lining to keep heat contained.
Refractory bricks withstand extreme temperatures.

senses.definition: the third-person singular present of refract; to bend light as it passes through a material.
examples: Water refracts light to create a rainbow.
The lens refracts rays to focus them on the retina.

senses.definition: capable of being refracted; able to bend light.
examples: Certain liquids are refracturable more than others.
The refracturable portion of the spectrum was analyzed.

senses.definition: to fracture again or a second fracture; to break or crack again after healing.
examples: The bone may refracture if stressed too soon after surgery.
If the cast is removed, the bone could refracture.

senses.definition: a fracture that occurs again after healing.
examples: The patient suffered a refracture after a fall.
X-rays showed a refracture at the original site.

senses.definition: past tense of refracture; broke again after healing.
examples: The bone refractured after the accident.
He refractured the tooth by biting hard.

senses.definition: plural of refracture; multiple secondary fractures.
examples: The patient has several refractures after the fall.
Refractures can complicate healing.

senses.definition: third-person plural present of refracture; they fracture again.
examples: The bones refracture more easily in this condition.
Poor healing can lead to refractures.

senses.definition: the process of refracturing; breaking again after healing.
examples: Refracturing of the materials is a concern in this design.
Refracturing can weaken the structure.

senses.definition: gerund of refracture; the act of fracturing again.
examples: The bone is refracturing after the impact.
We must monitor for refracturing during rehabilitation.

senses.definition: the quality of being refraggable; the ability to be refracted or broken apart and reassembled in theory.
examples: The refragability of the crystal is high.
This material demonstrates refragability under light.

senses.definition: capable of being refragable; able to be broken and reassembled in theory.
examples: The refragable crystal can be split and rejoined.
A refragable pattern was observed in the experiment.

senses.definition: the state or quality of being refragable; the ability to be refragable.
examples: Refragableness affects how the material behaves under light.
Assessing refragableness helps in material design.

senses.definition: to break or separate into fragments again; to fracture anew.
examples: The pottery was refragated into small shards.
The ancient vase refragate under pressure.

senses.definition: to fragment again; to break something into fragments again.
examples: To study the history, they refragmented the artifact.
The researchers refragmented the data into smaller groups.

senses.definition: to stop yourself from doing something; to hold back.
examples: Please refrain from talking during the lecture.
She refrains from sugar to stay healthy.

senses.definition: a line in a song that is repeated; a chorus.
examples: The refrain repeats the main idea of the song.
Each chorus serves as the refrain of the piece.

senses.definition: past tense of refrain; stopped oneself from doing something.
examples: She refrained from commenting on the issue.
They refrained to avoid conflict.

senses.definition: a person who refrains; someone who holds back or abstains.
examples: The refrainer chose not to speak.
A steady refrainer, he kept quiet during the debate.

senses.definition: plural of refrainer; people who refrain.
examples: The refrainers of the group avoided spicy food.
Several refrainers chose not to participate.

senses.definition: present participle of refrain; the act of holding back from doing something.
examples: She is refraining from making a judgment.
Refraining from anger helped the situation.

senses.definition: the act of refraining; restraint or self-control.
examples: Refrainment is important in diplomacy.
With refrainment, the argument ended peacefully.

senses.definition: plural of refrainment; acts of refraining or restraints.
examples: Several refrainments were noted during the negotiation.
The refrainments helped de-escalate the situation.

senses.definition: third-person singular present of refrain; to hold back from doing something.
examples: He refrains from making promises.
The city refrains from issuing a curfew.

senses.definition: a repeated line or chorus in a song or poem; a refrain.
examples: The refrain repeats the main idea of the song.
Listeners hummed the catchy refrain.

senses.definition: to present or consider something in a different or new way.
examples: We need to reframe the issue to find a solution.
The documentary reframes the historical event from a local perspective.

senses.definition: past tense of reframe; presented in a different way.
examples: The artist reframed the story through visuals.
She reframed her argument to focus on evidence.

senses.definition: third-person singular present of reframe; presents differently.
examples: The campaign reframes the issue for a broader audience.
It reframes the data to emphasize trends.

senses.definition: the act of reframing; changing the way something is viewed.
examples: Reframing helped the team see the problem clearly.
Cognitive reframing can reduce stress.
